title: Time Scaling
summary: This page explains time scaling of continuous-time signals, where replacing t with at compresses the signal if a > 1 and expands it if a < 1. Example 1.4 demonstrates time compression by factor 3 and time expansion by factor 2 applied to a piecewise signal x(t). The mathematical derivations show how the domain intervals transform under each scaling operation.

figures.caption: Illustration of time-scaling a continuous-time signal x(t): (a) original signal, (b) time-compressed version x(2t) with duration halved, and (c) time-expanded version x(t/2) with duration doubled.
figures.description: The figure has three stacked time-domain plots with horizontal axis t and vertical signal amplitude, showing a triangular continuous-time signal and its time-scaled versions. In (a), the original signal x(t) rises from zero at T1 to a peak at t=0 and returns to zero at T2; in (b), the compressed signal x(2t) has the same shape over the shorter interval from T1/2 to T2/2; in (c), the expanded signal x(t/2) stretches the same shape over the longer interval from 2T1 to 2T2. Students should notice that time scaling changes the signal duration along the t-axis while preserving the basic amplitude shape.
